did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly uses deep learning (U-net architecture) and unsupervised machine learning (Expectation Maximization algorithm) for skin lesion segmentation in dermatoscopy images to evaluate color spaces.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work evaluating the discrimination power of 5 color spaces and 16 color channels using unsupervised (Expectation Maximization) and deep learning (U-net) approaches on 600 dermatoscopy images. It includes specific sections for Materials and Methods, Results, and Discussion, and reports quantitative findings such as mean dice scores, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: EVALUATION OF COLOR SPACES FOR UNSUPERVISED AND DEEP LEARNING SKIN LESION SEGMENTATION
journal: International Conferences Computer Graphics, Visualization, Computer Vision and Image Processing 2020; Big Data Analytics, Data Mining and Computational Intelligence 2020; and Theory and Practice in Modern Computing 2020
brief_summary: This study evaluated the discrimination power of five color spaces and sixteen color channels for skin lesion segmentation in dermatoscopy images to address the lack of quantitative selection criteria. The researchers utilized 600 images from the ISIC archive and Hospital Pedro Hispano, categorized by artifacts such as hair occlusion and cluttering. Segmentation was performed using unsupervised Expectation Maximization and deep learning U-net models, with performance measured by the Dice similarity index. The results indicated that no single color space or channel was universally optimal across all image complexities. Consequently, the study proposes a general framework for selecting specific or combined color channels to enhance segmentation accuracy under varying illumination and scene conditions.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on the segmentation of 'skin lesion' in dermatoscopy images. The specific histopathological types (e.g., melanoma, nevus, BCC) are not specified or distinguished in the provided text; the dataset consists of 600 images from the ISIC archive and Hospital Pedro Hispano categorized by artifacts (normal, dark corners, hair occlusion, clutter, multiple artifacts) rather than by specific disease diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion region in dermatoscopy images
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates unsupervised (Expectation Maximization, SD+) and deep learning (U-net) algorithms for skin lesion segmentation. The models predict a binary mask separating the lesion (target object) from the background (healthy skin, clutter, occlusions). Performance is measured using the Dice similarity index.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: Images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration archive.

use.training: Part of the combined dataset used for training (500 images total).
use.testing: Part of the combined dataset used for testing (100 images total).

datasets_used.name: Hospital Pedro Hispano
datasets_used.description: Images from the Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano, Matosinhos, Portugal.

use.training: Part of the combined dataset used for training (500 images total).
use.testing: Part of the combined dataset used for testing (100 images total).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure the segmentation accuracy by comparing the predicted lesion region with the ground truth provided by certified dermatologists.
To compute the average segmentation performance across all test images for each color space and color channel to determine optimal channels.
To visualize the frequency distribution of dice scores and the number of times specific color spaces/channels were determined to be optimal across the test images.
To quantitatively evaluate the perceptual quality of the test images as a visual attribute influencing segmentation accuracy.
To calculate the probability that a specific color channel is the optimal color channel across the test images based on histogram counts.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics, specifically mean Dice similarity scores and histogram distributions of optimal color channels, to determine the most suitable color spaces and channels for segmentation.

ISIC Archive and Hospital Pedro Hispano.dataset_description: 600 dermatoscopy images (464 from ISIC, 136 from Hospital Pedro Hispano) categorized into 500 training and 100 test images. Test images classified by artifacts: normal (233), dark corners (84), hair occlusion (93), clutter (57), and multiple artifacts (133).
ISIC Archive and Hospital Pedro Hispano.evaluation_setting: Segmentation of skin lesions using unsupervised (Expectation Maximization, SD+) and deep learning (U-net) algorithms across 5 color spaces (RGB, HSV, CIELAB, YCbCr, XYZ) and 16 color channels.

SD+ (Unsupervised).dice_score_notes: Recorded a dice score lower than 0.80 for 10 percent of the images. For about 30 percent of the images, there was a dice score less than 0.9. Considered robust but with room for improvement.
SD+ (Unsupervised).notes: Optimized for the saturation channel of the HSV color space.

Expectation Maximization (EM) (Unsupervi.color_space_mean_dice: Average dice score for all color spaces was < 0.6.
Expectation Maximization (EM) (Unsupervi.optimal_color_channel: Blue channel (P ≈ 0.3, meaning it was optimal for ~30% of test images).
Expectation Maximization (EM) (Unsupervi.high_performance_channels: Red, green, blue, grayscale, saturation, luminance, and Y channels demonstrated remarkable performance.
Expectation Maximization (EM) (Unsupervi.notes: Performance limited by presence of clutters and occluding objects due to lack of hair removal pre-processing. No single color space was most suitable (no channel had P = 0.5).

U-net (Deep Learning).training_parameters: Stochastic gradient descent with momentum, learning rate 1 × 10^-3, 150 epochs, batch size 32.
U-net (Deep Learning).optimal_color_space: HSV (P ≈ 0.4, deemed optimal more times than other color spaces).
U-net (Deep Learning).performance_notes: Demonstrated robust performance for all color spaces except CIELAB and YCbCr. Did not repeat the same level of performance when operated using color channels, except for the Z color channel. Performed better with more information provided by color spaces.
U-net (Deep Learning).notes: No specific mean dice scores per channel were reported numerically, only relative performance comparisons and optimal channel probabilities.

ISIC Archive and Hospital Pedro Hispano.notes: The study concludes that no single color space or color channel is universally optimal. The discrimination power of a color channel depends on the level of clutter and occluding objects. A general framework for selecting single or combined color channels is proposed.

architecture_used.input_size: Not reported (Images had varying dimensions: rows 665-6668, columns 572-4439)

U-net.encoder: Two 3x3 convolutional layers with 64 filters, ReLU activation, and max pooling layer (downsamples input by factor of 2)
U-net.decoder: One 4x4 transposed convolutional layer with 64 filters (upsamples input by factor of 2)

Expectation Maximization.type: Iterative procedure modeling parameters for observed data
Expectation Maximization.steps: Alternates between expectation step (estimates missing data) and maximization step (maximizes likelihood function)

steps: Conversion of RGB to HSV and extraction of saturation channel
Detection of confounding factors (shading, specular reflections), inpainting, and preliminary segmentation
Morphological post-processing to refine binary mask and extract lesion region

Expectation Maximization.classes: Not explicitly stated, implied binary segmentation

preprocessing_techniques: Color space transformation (RGB to HSV, CIELAB, YCbCr, XYZ)
Color channel extraction (16 channels from 5 color spaces)
Inpainting (for confounding factors like shading and specular reflections in SD+ algorithm)
Morphological post-processing (for refining binary masks in SD+ algorithm)
Normalization of spatial extent metric using image area
conclusions: No single color space or color channel is universally optimal for skin lesion segmentation across real-world scenarios with varying scene complexities and illumination variations.
The discrimination power of a color channel depends on the level of cluttering and occluding objects present in the image.

The SD+ unsupervised algorithm is optimized for the saturation channel of the HSV color space but shows room for improvement, recording dice scores lower than 0.80 for 10% of images and lower than 0.9 for about 30% of images.

The Expectation Maximization (EM) unsupervised algorithm's performance is limited by clutters and occluding objects due to the lack of hair removal pre-processing, resulting in an average dice score below 0.6 across all color spaces.

For the EM algorithm, the blue channel was identified as the most frequently optimal color channel (P ≈ 0.3), while red, green, blue, grayscale, saturation, luminance, and Y channels demonstrated remarkable performance.
The U-net deep learning model demonstrated robust performance across most color spaces, with HSV being the optimal color space (P ≈ 0.4).
The U-net model performed better when utilizing the full information provided by color spaces rather than individual color channels, except for the Z color channel.

The study proposes a general framework for selecting single or combined color channels to enhance segmentation accuracy for images with different levels of scene complexities and illumination variations.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that the training and testing datasets (500 training, 100 test images) may not be large enough to fully optimize the segmentation models.
The U-net training parameters (learning rate 1e-3, 150 epochs, batch size 32) may not result in optimal segmentation performance.
The simplified post-processing applied to the output of the Expectation Maximization (EM) and U-net models may not optimize segmentation accuracy.
The Expectation Maximization (EM) algorithm lacks a hair removal pre-processing step, limiting its performance in the presence of clutters and occluding objects (average dice score < 0.6).
The SD+ algorithm was restricted to the saturation channel of the HSV color space and recorded dice scores lower than 0.80 for 10% of images and lower than 0.9 for about 30% of images.
No single color space or color channel was found to be universally optimal; performance depends heavily on the level of cluttering and occluding objects.
The U-net model demonstrated robust performance across most color spaces but performed poorly with CIELAB and YCbCr color spaces.
The study proposes a general framework rather than a specific optimized model, noting that limitations do not diminish the general concepts but affect specific optimization.
